speaker: interviewer
text: Max, I'm wondering if you'd be willing to share any more little stories about some of the things you said that you would never dare do today when you were a kid in those years.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mainly is to get food for the kids. Because like I said, we were about 10 kids, the bigger ones. And we provided for all the rest of it, mainly. We had food, but all the supplement came from what we were stealing. We had big capes, black capes, like the scout, with big pockets in there. And I remember it was a-- a grocery store. And, well, unfortunately, it was an older lady. And she lived upstairs on the second floor. So at that time when we went into the store, by the time she came down, we went two, three guys, who used to take jelly and whatever was available. And then we used to buy a pack of matches or cards. We-- we used to rob her blind [INAUDIBLE]. You know, when I think about it, it was awful, you know. This is one thing I remember.

speaker: interviewee
text: We used to go on trips. We went to the farmers. And we used to say, we are a bunch of children, and we are hungry. Could you give us something? So they-- they used to give us cheese, eggs.

speaker: interviewee
text: They-- they used to give us all kind of things, you know. So, but boys are boys. And we start to argue. One had more than the other and everything. So the poor eggs that we had, we start throwing at each other. We used to fight with the eggs, I remember. And then we were all crying because we didn't have no eggs no more. What we made over there, it's unbelievable, unbelievable. This-- this-- you know, she must have had a lot of courage, this woman, to take care of-- of those-- those children. I think they got paid, though. They got a certain amount of money from the underground for to take care of all those children. If not, they did it for a good heart, also. But they got a-- a monetary award out of it. This particular lady, from-- I forgot her name. But after the war, she got honored from the-- the Jewish federation. She got a plaque and everything, you know. That we went with-- the one from the first home lived to be a very old lady, Mrs. [INAUDIBLE]. And they honored her. And-- and I think she's in-- in Israel, Yad Vashem, over there. She has a plate over there, too. That's about all that-- that I can remember. We went-- we went to school. And we were pretty active. We played practically all day long. We used to play soccer with-- with a ball that fell apart in little tiny pieces. This ball kept on shrinking, shrinking, shrinking, you know, a-- a solid ball, not the soccer ball, but whatever we could put together. We were-- we kept busy. We kept busy.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there a special memory that you have of that whole time that stands out more than anything else, that you just really, really remember strongly?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the-- the best part is it when the American soldiers came in, when they-- they freed us, you know. Because we-- the soldiers were in Couvin. And-- we were in Cul-des-sarts. Cul-des-sarts is about, like I said, about 20 miles from the other one.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we went over there to meet Americans. And we met-- we met this-- this Jewish man over there. And we went back. We took him with us to the home from the children. That's why he met all the Jewish children.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a very strong impression for us, because he was going with his-- with his- his gun and-- we were carrying the gun for him. We were walking on the street with him. And we brought back an American soldier. I don't understand how they can get-- walk away from-- from the regiment there, you know. And then he went back. And then he came back with, like I told you, with an ambulance full of stuff. This was a good, good impression for us, when we got freed. Because we knew that, in particular, when my father came, then I knew they survived. And for sure, I was happy, yeah. Yeah. This is a good impression, that's for sure. That's about it. See, the-- the years went by, one, two, three, trying to remember. That's three years of my life that we were gone. But compared to the other countries, I think Belgium, the children didn't have that bad. Because we-- the-- the-- the people from Belgium collaborated to help the people. In other countries, they collaborated with the German to get rid of the people. It makes a big difference. Because in Belgium, the-- the people themselves didn't like the Germans. They were occupied in the First World War, and they didn't like it to be occupied the Second World War. So they were against the Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: Are you involved in the child survivor groups?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm a member. I'm a participant. I went-- last year, they had it in Belgium. So we specially went back to Belgium to participate there and to look up the-- the people. But it's so far away, you know, that my-- my-- it's already in my past, you know. I-- I meet people that I didn't see for 40, 50 years. And the-- the first 10 minutes, it's fine, I mean. And then, we have nothing in common. Because you met when you were a child, and all of a sudden, here's a big adult and an old man on top of it, you know, not young person.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they don't have interest in me, and I have no interest in them. And life goes on, see, except close friends that I kept in touch with all those years. Like, I have one friend of mine, I went back once to Belgium, and I walk to go to temple. And it was Rosh Hashanah.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I see a guy. And I turn around. I said, gee whiz. You look so familiar to me. And I-- I recognize him from during the war.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I went up to him, and I said, are you Henri de Smet? Because this was his name. He says, yes. How do you know? I say, you don't recognize me?

speaker: interviewee
text: And then we got together. And then we are friends till today. And he-- he organized this reunion from the people that remain from this home, him and another guy. Because there is about, I would say, about a dozen that remain in Belgium. And then a few people came. Some came from America. Some came from Canada. And that's why we had the reunion last year on the same time that there was the-- the reunion for the-- the Holocaust survivors children. That's why we got together. It was a big affair there. I went for three days, there. And I went to visit and see pictures and stuff like this. And whenever they have something, they send it. And if I can make it, I would go again.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, thank you for sharing your story, Max.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you very much. And I hope that I was some help for other people that would be able to learn something from this, too, and for me, too, and my family, for sure. Thank you very much, too. This is Nathan Wunderman. And that-- that's my father. And this is Hena. Her maiden name is [INAUDIBLE]. Hannah Wunderman. This is in 1924 on their wedding. And it's in Skierniewice, Poland. This is 1942. And this is my sister, Bella to the left. I am in the center. And to the right is my brother, Severin.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was the picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: And it's taken in Brussels, Belgium. This is also 1942. And this is a group of children that was in the home, the very first home that I was. This was Jamoigne. And I'm the second-- the second child to the right.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Jamoigne, in Belgium. This is also 1942. And that's my brother, Severin. And that's a picture taken in Brussels, Belgium.

speaker: interviewer
text: In? Oh, you said the date. OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is 1946. And that's me, Max Wunderman. And I'm in Hashomer Hatzair, in Brussels, Belgium.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is-- wait-- this is my daughter, Claudine Wunderman and-- and her husband, Kevin Lally. Her name is Claudine Lally. And should I say how old they are? Where was the picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: The-- the picture's taken in Los Angeles. In-- I don't remember. I don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: Approximately?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Los Angeles. That's about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Max, I would like for you to introduce your family to us.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. This is my wife, Helen. That's my son, Brian. That's my daughter Natalie. And that's my son-in-law Bradley.

speaker: interviewee
text: Would any of you like to say anything about the experience that you've heard your father go through and how it's affected your life, maybe, as a-- as a child of survivors? BRIAN (SON): I think hearing the stories of both my parents and the trials and tribulations that they've gone through has made all of us a little bit stronger people, that we will continue to go after what we want. And for that, I appreciate it.

speaker: interviewee
text: NATALIE (DAUGHTER): Well, I just that feel that every day of life is important. Don't take it for granted. If they weren't here, we wouldn't be here. And I appreciate that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you, all. NATALIE (DAUGHTER): Thank you.
